kanji.meaningHint: Make sure you look at this amazing fishing lure too - hold it in your hand, see the two barbs fused together. Think about how strange this lure is. Is this really the best in the world? Will this really finish all other fishing lures?
kanji.readingMnemonic: How does the fish lure maker test his newly <kanji>finish</kanji>ed lure out? The one he just finished? He gets in a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat and rows out into the sea.
kanji.readingHint: Make him think that he can’t just use his new lure on the side of the lake / ocean / whatever. He has to get out there, but unfortunately all he has is a crappy row boat. Imagine the row boat being terrible, leaky, and dangerous, which is why you’re surprised when he goes for it. That’s a terrible row boat to use! If you do this, you'll focus your mind on the rowboat part of things, which will help you to remember the reading of this kanji.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: You know that 助ける means "to help." This word, 助かる, is related, but it's a bit different. It means to be helped or to be saved. It's intransitive, so it doesn't take a direct object. Imagine shouting "I'm saved!" No direct object, right? Verbs ending in okurigana that rhyme with ある tend to be intransitive like this. vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
You've seen the word 化ける (to transform) before, and here's a related word: 化かす. This means <vocabulary>to bewitch</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to trick</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to deceive</vocabulary>.

Why? In Japanese folklore, foxes, spirits and other yokai often transform in order to bewitch, trick, or deceive humans. To 化かす them, in other words.

This verb is transitive, so it needs a direct object — you bewitch, trick, or deceive someone. You know this because when a fox wants to bewitch someone, they have to <reading>cuss</reading> (かす). They say a magic cuss word that lets them change forms and fool people. Those tricksy foxes…
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as 化ける, so make sure you know that word and you'll be able to learn this word's reading as well.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: You <kanji>exit</kanji> your house and you <kanji>meet</kanji> someone. You didn’t mean to meet them, but it happened anyway. That’s why this word means <vocabulary>to meet by chance</vocabulary>.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic:
The reading is that of the word 出る and the word 会う, both of which you've hopefully seen. The 出 portion may be confusing, but you can usually assume that if one part of the word is kun'yomi / vocab reading, the other part is too, which is why 出 uses the vocab reading.

Oh by the way, this word can be written like this too: 出合う. It’s not as common, but you may come across it every now and then, so it’s good to know!

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>reflect</kanji>. The verb vocab version means <vocabulary>to be reflected</vocabulary>. You're not doing the reflecting directly. You can remember this by thinking how rude (る) it is that someone's reflecting your image using a mirror for everyone outside the dressing room to see.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

Your reflection is getting reflected onto the mirror. You don't like it, it doesn't look like you. Impostor reflection! You take a hammer and you <reading>打つ</reading> (うつ) it as hard as you can, glass flying everywhere. Then some dubstep starts playing from the broken mirror: <reading>うつ</reading>うつうつうつ. 

You might recall that this reading is the same as <reading>写</reading>る and might wonder if they're related. They are! They both talk about the image of something showing up somewhere else, and use different kanji to distinguish where. In 写る's case, that would typically be in photographs, and for 映る, it's in reflections or projections (which includes the contents of screens, like those of TVs or computers).

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
What's the verb form of <kanji>request</kanji>? It's <vocabulary>to request something</vocabulary>. It can also be <vocabulary>to want something</vocabulary> or even <vocabulary>to demand something</vocabulary>.

求める means actively trying to obtain something, whether it's a thing, an answer, help, or even an ideal. The translation will change depending on context: アドバイスを求める (request advice), 助けを求める (ask for help), and 答えを求める (seek an answer) are all possible uses.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic:
This word has kanji with hiragana attached, so you can guess it'll use a kun'yomi reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember it:

You're going <vocabulary>to request something</vocabulary>, but what is it? It's a <reading>moat</reading> (もと). A moat around your house. In fact, you're going to demand one. Your neighbor's cats keep pooping on your lawn, but if you had a moat they wouldn't be able to get to your lawn in the first place, since cats hate water (and alligators). You demand a moat, damn it!
